description: Optimizing product search where 'metadata' JSONB column has become a de-facto schema. Review the index strategy, the denormalization, and the architectural decision to keep schemaless storage for queryable fields.
review_checklist: Evaluate GIN with default ops vs jsonb_path_ops for the actual query patterns
Identify when JSONB queryable fields should be promoted to columns
Check ALTER TABLE + UPDATE pattern at scale
Consider write-amplification of duplicate keyword storage
Audit whether the schemaless choice still serves the workload

ground_truth.description: GIN index without jsonb_path_ops creates a full inverted index over every key/value pair. Queries that only use containment (@>) work fine with the smaller jsonb_path_ops variant — the index is ~30% the size and ~2x faster to update. Use `USING gin(metadata jsonb_path_ops)` if queries are containment-only.

Plain UPDATE without batching on a 12M-row table acquires row-level locks on every row in a single transaction. WAL volume balloons (~8GB) and the migration holds locks for hours. Batch updates of 5-10K rows per transaction with brief commits.

Architectural smell: brand/category/color are queryable, indexed, and central to search — they should be first-class columns, not JSON keys. Use of JSONB for high-cardinality, frequently-queried, schema-stable fields gives up: type checking, NOT NULL enforcement, foreign keys, query planner statistics, and pg_stat insights. Promote to columns; keep JSONB for genuinely variable per-product attributes.

searchable_keywords is a denormalized projection of metadata. Every product update now requires updating both columns — application logic must remain in sync indefinitely or use a generated column. Use GENERATED ALWAYS AS (...) STORED to enforce derivation.

Both indexes (idx_products_metadata, idx_products_keywords) cover overlapping query needs. Pick one. Two GIN indexes on the same table double write cost on every UPDATE and waste ~6GB of buffer cache.
